title: Display Data: Histograms
contentObjective: I can make and read a histogram to display data in intervals.
languageObjective: I can explain my histogram using the words histogram, frequency, interval, and distribution.

turnAndTalk.question: You have 30 players' points-per-game averages. Why is it easier to display them in a histogram with intervals than to list all 30 numbers?
turnAndTalk.kernel: A histogram groups data into equal intervals so large data sets become readable.

stems.en: A histogram is easier because it groups the data into ___.
stems.es: Un histograma es más fácil porque agrupa los datos en ___.

stems.en: Each bar shows how many players fall in that ___.
stems.es: Cada barra muestra cuántos jugadores caen en ese ___.

turnAndTalk.listenFor: Students explain grouping into intervals makes 30 values readable and that each bar's height is the frequency for that interval.
turnAndTalk.extend: Predict: how would the histogram change if you used intervals of 5 instead of 10? Justify the trade-off.
extendStems: With intervals of 5, there would be ___ bars and ___ detail.
Smaller intervals show ___ but can make the shape look ___.

turnAndTalk.question: As you build the frequency table with intervals of 10, what does the height of each bar in the histogram represent?
turnAndTalk.kernel: The height of a histogram bar is the frequency of values in that interval.

stems.en: The height of each bar shows the ___ of that interval.
stems.es: La altura de cada barra muestra la ___ de ese intervalo.

stems.en: The tallest bar is the interval where ___.
stems.es: La barra más alta es el intervalo donde ___.

turnAndTalk.listenFor: A strong answer states the bar height equals how many players' averages fall in that interval (the frequency) and locates the tallest bar.
turnAndTalk.extend: Why can't you tell a single player's exact average just from the histogram? Justify using the intervals.
extendStems: I can't find one player's exact value because the bar only shows ___.
The histogram groups values, so I lose ___.

turnAndTalk.question: A histogram of shots-per-game shows 0-2: 1 player, 3-5: 8, 6-8: 10, 9-11: 3, 12-14: 1. The coach says 'most players take a good number of shots.' Does the shape support that claim?
turnAndTalk.kernel: The shape of a histogram shows where most of the data is concentrated.

stems.en: The histogram ___ the coach's claim because most players are in the ___ intervals.
stems.es: El histograma ___ la afirmación porque la mayoría está en los intervalos ___.

stems.en: The shape shows the data clusters around ___ shots.
stems.es: La forma muestra que los datos se agrupan alrededor de ___ tiros.

turnAndTalk.listenFor: Students read the 3-5 and 6-8 intervals (18 of 23 players) as the cluster and judge whether 'a good number' fairly describes the shape.
turnAndTalk.extend: Critique: a teammate ignores the 0-2 and 12-14 bars because they are short. Should those intervals be ignored? Defend.
extendStems: The short bars ___ be ignored because they still show ___.
Even one player in the 12-14 interval tells us ___.

turnAndTalk.question: A histogram of heights shows 60-63 in: 2, 64-67 in: 7, 68-71 in: 9, 72-75 in: 4. Which interval has the most players, and how do you know?
turnAndTalk.kernel: The tallest bar marks the interval with the greatest frequency.

stems.en: The ___ interval has the most players because its bar is ___.
stems.es: El intervalo ___ tiene más jugadores porque su barra es ___.

stems.en: I know because its frequency is ___.
stems.es: Lo sé porque su frecuencia es ___.

turnAndTalk.listenFor: Students identify 68-71 in (9 players) as the most-populated interval because it has the highest frequency.
turnAndTalk.extend: Generalize: what is one thing a histogram shows well, and one thing it cannot show, compared to a list of the raw data?
extendStems: It cannot show ___ because the data is grouped into ___.

vocabulary.definition: A bar graph that groups data into equal ranges. The bars touch.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Una gráfica de barras que agrupa datos en rangos iguales. Las barras se tocan.
vocabulary.visual: Bars side by side: 0-9 pts (3 players), 10-19 pts (8 players), 20-29 pts (4 players)
vocabulary.cloze: A bar graph that shows how often data falls into equal intervals is a ___.

examples.text: A graph with bars for the ranges 0–9, 10–19, 20–29
examples.why: It groups data into equal intervals.

examples.text: A graph comparing favorite colors with gaps between bars
examples.why: That is a bar graph of categories, not a histogram.

vocabulary.definition: How many times a value shows up.
vocabulary.visual: If 5 players scored 10-19 points, the frequency for that interval is 5
vocabulary.cloze: How many data values fall in an interval is the ___.

vocabulary.definition: A range of numbers used to group data.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Un rango de números para agrupar datos.
vocabulary.visual: 0-9, 10-19, 20-29 are intervals of width 10 — each covers 10 values
vocabulary.cloze: An equal-sized range of values used to group data is an ___.

vocabulary.definition: How the data is spread out.
vocabulary.visual: Most data in the middle with fewer at the ends = bell-shaped; most on one side with a tail = skewed
vocabulary.cloze: The overall pattern of how data is spread out is the ___.

vocabulary.definition: How the data looks: where it sits and how spread out it is.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Cómo se ven los datos: dónde están y qué tan separados están.
vocabulary.visual: A histogram that is tallest in the middle and shorter on both sides is symmetric
vocabulary.cloze: The way data values are spread out is the ___.

vocabulary.definition: How spread out the numbers are.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Qué tan separados están los números.
vocabulary.visual: Data in just 2 intervals = low variability. Data across 6 intervals = high variability
vocabulary.cloze: How much the data values differ is the ___.

launch.narrative: The basketball league has 30 players and wants to display everyone's points-per-game average in a way that shows how the data is distributed. The raw averages are: 2, 5, 7, 8, 9, 10, 11, 11, 12, 13, 13, 14, 14, 15, 15, 16, 16, 17, 18, 18, 19, 20, 21, 22, 23, 24, 25, 28, 30, 32. Listing all 30 numbers is hard to read — a histogram will reveal the pattern!
launch.contextImage: League scoreboard with 30 player names and scoring averages

visual.title: League Scoreboard — Points Per Game (30 players)
visual.unit: Each tile is one player's scoring average.

noticePrompts: Where do most of the scoring averages seem to cluster?
Are there many players at the very low or very high end?
How could you group these numbers to see a pattern?
wonderPrompts: What size intervals would work best for this data?
What shape will the data make when grouped into a histogram?

explore.label: Create a frequency table for the league scoring data using intervals of 10. Then describe the histogram shape.

histogram.title: League Scoring Averages — Histogram (intervals of 10)
histogram.caption: The bars touch because the intervals are continuous. The tallest bar is the 10–19 interval.

discourse.prompt: The tallest bar is the 10–19 interval with 16 players. What does the shape of this histogram tell you about scoring in the league?
discourse.sentenceFrame: Most players score between ___ and ___ points per game. The histogram is skewed ___ because ___. This tells me that ___.

optional.stem: A histogram differs from a bar graph because...
choices: It groups numeric data into equal intervals with no gaps
optional.explanation: Histograms display continuous numeric data in intervals (bins) with bars touching.

optional.stem: A histogram bin for 10–19 has a bar height of 6. What does 6 represent?
choices: 6 data values fall in that interval
optional.explanation: The bar height is the frequency — how many values fall in that bin.

optional.stem: Histogram bins are 0–9, 10–19, 20–29 with heights 4, 9, 2. How many data values total?
Why should histogram intervals be equal in width?
choices: So the bars fairly compare how many values fall in each range
optional.explanation: Equal widths make frequency comparisons fair across bins.

approaching.stem: A histogram shows these frequencies: 0–4: 3 players, 5–9: 8 players, 10–14: 12 players, 15–19: 5 players. How many players are represented in total?
In a histogram, what does the height of each bar represent?
choices: The frequency (count) of data in that interval
The average of the data in that interval
approaching.explanation: Each bar's height shows the frequency — how many data values fall within that interval.

approaching.stem: How is a histogram different from a regular bar graph?
choices: Histogram bars touch (no gaps) because the intervals are continuous ranges
Histograms use colors and bar graphs don't
Bar graphs show numbers and histograms show words
approaching.explanation: In a histogram, bars touch because each interval connects to the next (0–9, 10–19, etc.) — the data is continuous.

approaching.stem: A histogram has intervals: 0–4, 5–9, 10–14, 15–19. What is the width of each interval?
approaching.explanation: Each interval covers 5 values (0,1,2,3,4 then 5,6,7,8,9 etc.), so the width is 5.

approaching.instructions: Put the steps for creating a histogram in the correct order.

categories.label: Steps (in order)
items: Step 1: Choose equal-sized intervals that cover all the data
Step 2: Count how many data values fall in each interval (frequency)
Step 3: Draw bars for each interval — height = frequency
Step 4: Make sure bars touch with no gaps

hints: You must decide on intervals before counting data in each one.
Histogram bars always touch because the data ranges are continuous.
approaching.label: Order the steps for making a histogram.

approaching.stem: A histogram bar for the interval 20–29 reaches a height of 7. What does the 7 tell you?
approaching.explanation: A bar's height is the frequency — here, 7 values land in the 20–29 interval.

approaching.label: Match each word to what it means on a histogram.

pairs.match: How many values are in an interval (the bar height)
Why a histogram has no gaps between bars

onLevel.stem: A histogram of test scores shows: 50–59: 2, 60–69: 5, 70–79: 10, 80–89: 8, 90–99: 3. Which interval has the most students?
onLevel.explanation: The 70–79 interval has the highest frequency of 10 students.

pairs.match: Bars touch, shows frequency by interval
Count of values in an interval

onLevel.label: Match each term to its description.

onLevel.instructions: Based on the histogram frequencies below, sort the intervals from most players to fewest: 0–9: 4, 10–19: 11, 20–29: 7, 30–39: 2.
hints: The tallest bar (most players) goes first.
onLevel.label: Rank the intervals from highest to lowest frequency.

onLevel.stem: A histogram of goals-per-game shows: 0–1: 9 players, 2–3: 6, 4–5: 3, 6–7: 1. What shape is this distribution?
choices: Skewed right (most players low, tail toward high values)
Skewed left (most players high, tail toward low values)
onLevel.explanation: The tallest bars are at the low end (0–1) and the bars shrink as goals increase — the tail points right, so it is skewed right.

onLevel.label: Use this data set of rebounds per game to complete the frequency table (intervals of 5): 1, 3, 4, 6, 7, 8, 9, 11, 12, 13, 14, 16.

extending.title: Find the Histogram Error

workedExample.label: Conclusion
workedExample.work: The histogram is symmetric because the bars go 1, 2, 3, 4, 2.

extending.correctWork: The frequencies are correct (1, 2, 3, 4, 2), but this is NOT symmetric. In a symmetric histogram, the bars rise and fall evenly from the center. Here the bars increase from left to right and only drop at the end — the data is skewed LEFT (most scores are on the higher end).
hints: Symmetric means the left side mirrors the right side. Does 1, 2, 3, 4, 2 mirror evenly?
The peak (4) is in the 80–89 range, which is toward the right. That means most data is on the high end.

extending.prompt: A teacher collected test scores and made two different histograms — one with intervals of 5 (50–54, 55–59, etc.) and one with intervals of 20 (50–69, 70–89, 90–109). Both use the same data. How might the two histograms look different? Which interval size gives you more detail about the distribution? When might the larger interval be better?
extending.sentenceFrame: The histogram with intervals of 5 would have ___ bars and show ___. The histogram with intervals of 20 would have ___ bars and show ___. Smaller intervals are better when ___, and larger intervals are better when ___.

extending.instructions: Drag each sports scenario under the histogram SHAPE its data would make.

categories.label: Symmetric (peak in the middle)
items: Player heights on a team — most are average height, fewer are very tall or very short

categories.label: Skewed Right (tail points to high values)
items: Points per game — most players score low, a few stars score very high

categories.label: Skewed Left (tail points to low values)
items: Free-throw % of experienced players — most are high, a few are very low

hints: Symmetric: the tallest bars are in the middle and the two sides mirror each other.
The 'skew' direction is where the long tail points — toward the few unusual values, not the cluster.
extending.label: Match each scenario to its histogram shape.

extending.stem: A histogram of points-per-game has its tallest bars on the LEFT (low scores) and a long tail stretching RIGHT toward a few high scorers. How is this distribution described?
extending.explanation: The tail points right (toward the high values), so it is skewed right — most players are clustered at the low end.

extending.stem: Two histograms show the SAME 40 test scores — one uses intervals of 5, the other intervals of 20. Which statement is true?
choices: The interval-of-5 histogram has more bars and shows more detail about the shape
The interval-of-20 histogram has more bars
Both histograms must look exactly the same
The interval-of-5 histogram leaves some scores out
extending.explanation: Smaller intervals create more bars and reveal finer detail; larger intervals smooth the shape into fewer bars. Both still include all 40 scores.

connect.scenario: A soccer coach looks at a histogram of shots-per-game for the team: 0–2 shots: 1 player, 3–5 shots: 8 players, 6–8 shots: 10 players, 9–11 shots: 3 players, 12–14 shots: 1 player. The coach says, 'Most of my players take a good number of shots.'

histogram.title: Shots Per Game — Team Histogram
histogram.caption: Read the shape: where do most players cluster?

connect.promptQuestion: Does the histogram support the coach's claim? What does the shape of the data tell you?
connect.prompt: The histogram peaks at the ___ interval with ___ players. The shape is approximately ___. This ___ the coach's claim because ___.

exitTicket.stem: A histogram of player heights shows: 60–63 in: 2, 64–67 in: 7, 68–71 in: 9, 72–75 in: 4. Which interval has the most players?
exitTicket.explanation: The 68–71 inch interval has the highest frequency of 9 players.
